metadata.subject: Arts Education
metadata.description: Dance Foundations 10 introduces students to the foundational technical and expressive skills of dance as an art form. Students develop their bodies as expressive instruments, explore a range of dance styles and choreographic tools, and engage with dance as a means of communicating identity, culture, and personal meaning. First Peoples dance traditions and diverse global dance forms are explored with cultural respect and curiosity. This course opens pathways into Dance Technique and Performance, Dance Choreography, and Dance Company.
metadata.general_description: A foundational senior dance course developing technique, body awareness, movement vocabulary, choreographic skills, and cultural understanding — introducing students to dance as an expressive art form and opening pathways to advanced dance study.

big_ideas.statement: Dance offers unique ways of exploring our identity and sense of belonging.
big_ideas.elaboration: Movement is one of the most fundamental ways humans express who they are and where they come from. Dance is a physical language that connects body, community, and culture.

big_ideas.statement: Individual and collective expression is rooted in history, culture, community, and values.
big_ideas.elaboration: Dance forms are not created in isolation — they emerge from and reflect the communities, histories, and values of the people who dance them. Understanding this context deepens both technique and appreciation.

big_ideas.statement: Growth as a dancer and choreographer requires perseverance, resilience, and reflection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Dance development is a long-term process requiring consistent practice, constructive self-criticism, openness to feedback, and the courage to perform and create in front of others.

big_ideas.statement: The body is an instrument for artistic expression in dance.
big_ideas.elaboration: Just as a musician develops their instrument, a dancer develops their body — cultivating strength, flexibility, coordination, spatial awareness, and expressive range to convey meaning through movement.

big_ideas.statement: Traditions, perspectives, worldviews, and stories are shared through aesthetic dance experiences.
big_ideas.elaboration: Dance carries cultural memory and transmits values, histories, and spiritual knowledge across generations and communities. Engaging with diverse dance traditions builds cross-cultural understanding and respect.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate dance elements and techniques in various historical and contemporary styles; develop body as expressive instrument; explore interplay of movement, sound, image, and form to convey meaning; express meaning, intent, and emotion through movement; select dance elements and techniques to create innovative movement phrases; create and perform phrases individually and collaboratively; use choreographic devices to generate movement; take creative risks; consider audience and venue while composing and performing.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate foundational technique and elements in various dance styles.
I can develop my body as a precise and expressive instrument for artistic communication.
I can create movement phrases individually and collaboratively using choreographic devices.
I can take creative risks in composing and performing dance, considering the audience and performance context.
I can use the interplay of movement, sound, image, and form to express specific meanings and emotions.

competencies.raw: Describe and respond to movement using dance vocabulary; reflect on rehearsal and performance experiences; receive and apply constructive feedback; demonstrate respect for self, others, audience, and place.
i_can_statements: I can describe and analyze movement using accurate dance vocabulary.
I can reflect meaningfully on my rehearsal and performance experiences to guide artistic growth.
I can receive constructive feedback and apply it to improve my dancing and choreography.
I can demonstrate respect for myself, my fellow dancers, my audience, and the spaces I dance in.

competencies.raw: Document, share, and appreciate dance works in various contexts; perform exercises, steps, and combinations in varied contexts; develop strategies to communicate ideas and emotions through dance; use dance to address personal and social issues; express identity and values through movement compositions.
i_can_statements: I can document and share dance works through video, notation, journals, and performance.
I can develop movement strategies to communicate specific ideas, emotions, and personal values.
I can express my identity, cultural background, and values through original movement compositions.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate responsibility in creating, performing, and responding to dance; explore local, national, global, and intercultural performers and genres; explore First Peoples perspectives and cultural knowledge through movement; examine how dance impacts cultures and societies; build personal and community connections through dance.
i_can_statements: I can demonstrate responsibility and professionalism in rehearsal, performance, and audience contexts.
I can explore and discuss local, national, global, and Indigenous dance traditions with respect and curiosity.
I can engage with First Peoples dance knowledge and perspectives thoughtfully and with appropriate protocols.
I can examine how dance reflects and shapes cultural values, community identity, and social change.

areas.name: Dance Elements and Technique
areas.raw: Elements of dance: body, space, time, dynamics, relationships; style-specific technique.
topics: Elements of dance: body (what moves), space (where it moves), time (when/how fast), dynamics (quality of movement), relationships (with others and environment)
Alignment and anatomical awareness: neutral spine, weight distribution, injury prevention
Technique skills: balance, weight transfer, flexibility, strength, coordination, and spatial awareness
Style-specific technique: foundations of contemporary, hip hop, and/or cultural dance forms
Warming up and cooling down: importance and practice

areas.name: Choreographic Skills
topics: Choreographic devices: repetition, contrast, accumulation, retrograde, unison, and canon
Choreographic forms: AB, ABA, rondo, theme and variation, narrative, and collage
Improvisation as a choreographic tool
Creating movement from stimuli: images, music, words, emotions, and objects
Rehearsal process: setting, memorizing, and refining choreography

areas.name: Performance and Presentation
areas.raw: Rehearsal and performance skills; technical, expressive, and cognitive competencies.
topics: Performance preparation: technical rehearsal, spacing, and stage awareness
Stage vocabulary: downstage, upstage, stage left/right, wings
Performance presence: projection, focus, and expressive commitment
Roles in dance: dancer, choreographer, director, audience, and critic
Dance documentation: journaling, video analysis, and self-reflection

areas.name: Cultural and Historical Contexts
areas.raw: Dance innovators; First Peoples worldviews; genre history; cultural appropriation ethics.
topics: First Peoples dance traditions: cultural significance, protocols, and respectful engagement
Dance history and genre development: modern, contemporary, jazz, hip hop, ballet
Influential dance innovators: Isadora Duncan, Martha Graham, Alvin Ailey, and others
Dance across cultures: traditional and contemporary forms from diverse communities
Cultural appropriation in dance: what it means, why it matters, and ethical alternatives
